title: Two teens learn the hard way not to do toy gun drive-bys from a Waymo
gist: Two California teens shooting gel-bead pellets from a moving Waymo robotaxi triggered the vehicle's internal cameras, which flagged what looked like a real gun; Waymo stopped the car, called San Mateo Police, and had them detained—illustrating that Waymo actively monitors in-cabin video, enforces rider rules regardless of local law, and has no presumption of passenger privacy.

insights.claim: Waymo's internal cameras flagged a black-painted SplatRBall gel-bead shooter as a possible real gun, prompting the robotaxi to stop and alert San Mateo Police, who detained the passengers.
insights.speaker: Jonathan M. Gitlin
insights.so_what: Demonstrates Waymo's operational capability to detect and act on in-cabin threats in real time—a differentiator and liability-management feature for the robotaxi model.
insights.quote: it obviously looked enough like a real gun when seen via the robotaxi's internal cameras to set off alarms.
insights.locator: And while the teens might have known

insights.claim: Waymo may review in-car video under certain circumstances and Support can access live video during a trip in urgent cases, meaning there is no presumption of privacy while riding.
insights.speaker: Jonathan M. Gitlin
insights.so_what: Persistent in-cabin monitoring is a structural feature of the robotaxi product—relevant to privacy regulation, consumer trust, and safety liability.
insights.quote: in more urgent circumstances, Support may access live video during a trip,” so there is no presumption of privacy while riding in one.
insights.locator: Waymo says it may review in-car video

insights.claim: This is not the first time a Waymo robotaxi reported misbehaving passengers—two men in Los Angeles were reported to police last year for drinking inside a robotaxi.
insights.speaker: Jonathan M. Gitlin
insights.so_what: A repeat pattern signals Waymo's enforcement behavior is systematic policy, not a one-off, shaping rider expectations and brand positioning.
insights.quote: According to Reddit, last year two men in Los Angeles were reported to the police for drinking inside a robotaxi.

themes.theme: Rider rules stricter than local law

insights.claim: Waymo prohibits all drug or alcohol use inside its robotaxis regardless of whether local law permits it, weapons, throwing objects from the car, and unaccompanied riders under 18.
insights.speaker: Jonathan M. Gitlin
insights.so_what: Waymo self-imposes conduct rules above statutory minimums, a risk-management posture that could become an industry norm for autonomous ride-hailing.
insights.quote: No drug or alcohol use is allowed inside its robotaxis, “regardless of whether permitted by local law.”
insights.locator: But Waymo's rules are quite clear

insights.claim: In California, adult taxi passengers are exempt from open-container laws that ban vehicle occupants from drinking in motion, but ride-hailing passengers get no such dispensation.
insights.speaker: Jonathan M. Gitlin
insights.so_what: The regulatory asymmetry between taxis and ride-hailing shapes how autonomous operators must set conduct policy to manage legal exposure.
insights.quote: in California they are not subject to the state's open container laws that otherwise ban occupants of a vehicle from consuming alcohol while in motion. Interestingly, ride-hailing passengers get no such dispensation.
